- The N bar (node 2) now has only 5 pixels and a different shape - it went from a horizontal bar to something smaller
- The red dot (node 4) moved from [10, 31] to [10, 28] - it shifted left
- The N shapes inside the container (nodes 13 and 14) are still there with 7 pixels each
- The Y bar (node 16) is still at 23 pixels
- The Y shapes inside the bottom container (nodes 20 and 23) are still there with 7 pixels each
So clicking on the N shape at [21, 39] (which is inside the left container of the top structure) caused the N bar to shrink. The N bar was 14 pixels and is now 5 pixels (lost 9 pixels). But the N shape inside the container still has 7 pixels.
Wait, let me reconsider. The N bar originally had 14 pixels (a 3x5 horizontal bar minus the red dot = 14). After clicking, it has 5 pixels. The red dot moved from [10, 31] to [10, 28].
Looking at the ascii:
- Before: `GNNNNN` (row 9), `GNNNrN` (row 10), `GNNNNN` (row 11) - the N bar was 3x5 with a red dot inside
- After: `GNN` (row 9), `GrN` (row 10), `GNN` (row 11) - the N bar is now 3x2 with a red dot
So the N bar shrank from 5 wide to 2 wide. The red dot is still inside it but shifted.
This is interesting. The N bar seems to be "consumed" by clicking on N shapes inside containers. Each N shape has 7 pixels, and the bar lost 9 pixels (14-5=9). But 7 != 9...
Wait, let me reconsider. The N bar was 14 pixels. After clicking one N shape (7 pixels), it became 5 pixels. So 14 - 9 = 5. But the N shape has 7 pixels, not 9. Unless the bar also lost the red dot's position...
Actually, looking more carefully:
- Original N bar: 3x5 = 15 cells, minus 1 for red dot = 14 N pixels
- After: the bar is now 3x2 = 6 cells, minus 1 for red dot = 5 N pixels
- Lost: 14 - 5 = 9 N pixels
But the N shape clicked had 7 pixels. So 9 != 7. Hmm.
Actually wait - looking at the boundary of node 2 after the click: `[[9, 28], [9, 29], [11, 29], [11, 28], [10, 29]]` - this is a 3x2 rectangle with an extra point. So it's 5 N pixels.
The original N bar boundary was `[[9, 28], [9, 32], [11, 32], [11, 28]]` - a 3x5 rectangle. That's 15 cells, minus 1 for the red dot = 14 N pixels.
So the bar went from 3x5 to 3x2, losing 9 cells. But the clicked N shape had 7 pixels. The difference is 2, which might be the width of the bar itself (3-1=2 for the G bar next to it?).
Hmm, let me think about this differently. Maybe the mechanic is:
- Each colored bar corresponds to colored shapes inside containers
- Clicking a shape "consumes" part of the matching bar
- The goal might be to consume all bars completely
Let me try clicking the other N shape (node 14) to see if the bar shrinks further, and also try clicking Y shapes to affect the Y bar.
